risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the allocation sequence will be comprised of computer-generated random numbers in a block design" and "Envelopes for randomisation will be stratified for risk of brain injury on routine cranial ultrasound". Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random sequence with block randomization and stratification, which is sufficient to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

Quote: "Randomisation will occur from concealed envelopes opened in front of the participants by non-study personnel. Treatment allocation will be recorded on a piece of folded paper inside each envelope in random order..." Comment: This explicitly states the allocation was concealed using concealed envelopes, opened by non-study personnel, which is a mechanism intended to prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. Although the envelopes are not described as opaque/sealed/sequentially numbered, the text does explicitly state they were concealed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the design of the study participants and intervention delivery facilitators will be informed of group allocation." Comment: Participants and those delivering the intervention were explicitly not blinded, and the main outcomes include parent-report child behavioural/emotional measures and parent outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, so performance bias is high for the study overall.

Quote: "Therapists conducting the BSID III assessment at 2 years C.A, and coders of the video/audio recorded observations will be masked to group allocation." Comment: The study explicitly states that the assessors of the objective developmental outcome (Bayley/BSID III) and the coders of the recorded observations were masked to allocation. The main primary outcome (ITSEA) is parent-report, so the parent is the assessor; however the outcome-assessment blinding question here is addressed for the clinician-rated and coded outcomes, which were blinded, and the objective Bayley III assessment is unlikely to be influenced by allocation knowledge.

Quote: "The sample size needs to be sufficient... expected retention rate is > 85% so that 330 families... will be recruited to allow for attrition." and "Families who identify at recruitment that they are unwilling to return to the hospital for the outcome assessment at 24 months C.A." Comment: Although this is a study protocol rather than a results paper, it indicates that the investigators anticipated good retention (>85%) and excluded only families unwilling to return for the final assessment, suggesting follow-up was planned to be largely complete and attrition was not expected to be substantial or obviously differential across groups. No evidence is provided of imbalanced or outcome-related missing data that would suggest attrition bias.
